12: 'All things are lawful for me' (panta moi exestin) appears twice in 1 Corinthians 6 (1 Corinthians 6:12, 13) and twice in 1 Corinthians 10 (1 Corinthians 6:23, 24) — likely a Corinthian slogan that Paul quotes then qualifies. The slogan may originate from a misappropriation of Paul's own teaching on freedom from the law (Galatians 5:1-6). The Corinthian pneumatics may have distinguished the body (morally irrelevant, destined for destruction) from the spirit (the locus of salvation) — allowing bodily indulgence as spiritually inconsequential. Paul's refutation uses the body-resurrection theology of 1 Corinthians 15 as its foundation: because the body will be raised, what you do with it matters eschatologically.
1: The lawsuit problem: in Roman Corinth, the civil court system (iudicia publica) was the standard venue for resolving disputes between private citizens. The Corinthian community apparently used these courts to settle disputes between members. Paul characterizes the judges as adikoi (unrighteous/unjust) — using the same term that will appear in the vice list of 1 Corinthians 6:9-10. The irony: the Corinthians are taking matters before judges whom Paul characterizes by the very vices being judged.
2: The eschatological role of the saints in judging the world and angels was a standard Second Temple Jewish expectation. The Qumran community expected to participate in God's final judgment (1QpHab 1 Corinthians 5:4; 4Q177); Wisdom of Solomon 3:8 declares the righteous will 'govern nations and rule over peoples.' Paul invokes this eschatological judicial role to argue by contrast: if believers are destined to judge the cosmos, how absurd that they cannot handle trivial commercial disputes within the community without appealing to pagan courts.
3: The judgment of angels adds a further eschatological dimension. In Second Temple Judaism, fallen angels were subject to final judgment (1 Enoch 10; 1 Corinthians 91:15; Jude 6) — and the righteous would participate in or witness this judgment. The reference here is not to the service of guardian angels but to the judgment of rebellious spiritual powers. Paul's logic is a fortiori: if believers will judge cosmic powers, surely they can judge biōtika (matters of everyday life) — a term implying the mundane and trivial compared to the cosmic.
4: The ironic recommendation to appoint the 'least esteemed' (exouthenēmenoi) as arbiters is a pointed social reversal. In Corinthian social culture, the most esteemed persons — those of high social standing, education, and rhetorical ability — were the expected arbiters of disputes. Paul inverts the honor hierarchy: if the Corinthians must have arbiters, why not use those the world regards as of no account? The reversal echoes the cross-wisdom of 1 Corinthians 1-2: God uses the foolish and weak to shame the wise and strong.
5: The shame-appeal ('is there not a wise person among you?') deploys Corinthian social values against their behavior. The Corinthians prized sophia (wisdom) and gnōsis (knowledge) as markers of spiritual status. To lack a person wise enough to arbitrate between brothers is a profound failure by the community's own standards. Paul's irony is pointed: the community that boasts of its wisdom cannot handle a civil dispute without recourse to pagan judges.
6: Brother suing brother epi apistōn (before unbelievers) — the phrase's shaming force depends on the social context where communal disputes before outsiders advertised internal division. In both Jewish communities (Josephus, Against Apion 2.193-194) and Greco-Roman associations, internal dispute resolution was expected precisely to avoid the public shame of airing community conflicts before outsiders. Paul's rebuke operates entirely within this social logic: the Corinthians' resort to public courts is a communal shaming act, regardless of who wins.
7: Paul's recommendation — 'why not rather suffer wrong? why not rather be defrauded?' — draws on a philosophical tradition of equanimity under injustice. The Stoics (Epictetus, Discourses 4.5) and Cynics celebrated the ability to absorb insult and injustice without retaliation as a mark of genuine freedom. More directly, Jesus's teaching (Matthew 5:39-40; Luke 6:29-30) forms the background: the willingness to absorb loss rather than seek legal redress was a mark of the community's distinctive ethos in contrast to honor-competition culture.
8: The accusation that Corinthians are themselves wronging and defrauding (adikeite kai apostereite) — not just being wronged — indicts both plaintiffs and defendants in the lawsuit culture. Commercial fraud and sharp practice (pleonexia) were pervasive in Corinthian trade; the archaeological record of Corinth as a major commercial hub (pottery, bronze, textiles, harbor fees) gives material texture to Paul's concern. The community is reproducing the commercial ethics of the marketplace rather than the counter-cultural economics of the Sermon on the Mount.
9: The vice list in 1 Corinthians 6:9-10 contains two terms related to same-sex conduct: malakoi (soft, effeminate — men who took the passive role in male-male intercourse) and arsenokoitai (males who lie with males — Paul's compound drawn from the LXX of Leviticus 18:22 and 1 Corinthians 20:13). In the Roman context, male-male sexual activity was widespread and legally unregulated, but the active/passive distinction was socially loaded: free male citizens were expected to be active, not passive (malakos status was shameful). Paul's condemnation covers both roles without the Roman active/passive moral distinction — both exclude from the kingdom.
10: The full vice list — sexually immoral, idolaters, adulterers, soft (malakoi), males-lying-with-males, thieves, greedy, drunkards, revilers, swindlers — maps onto the social vices of commercial Corinth. The pleonektai (greedy/covetous) and harpakes (swindlers/extortioners) are commercial terms that speak directly to the marketplace behavior Paul criticized in 1 Corinthians 6:8. The loidoroi (revilers) are relevant to the honor-competition culture of the Corinthian elite where verbal attacks on rivals were a normal form of social competition. The list is not abstract but addresses recognizable Corinthian social vices.
11: The three-fold transformation formula — washed, sanctified, justified (apeloysasthe, hēgiasthēte, edikaioōthēte) — describes the social transition from pagan community membership to the church community. The 'washing' likely refers to baptism, which in the ancient world had a strong social-initiation dimension (cf. proselyte baptism in Second Temple Judaism; ritual washing at Qumran for community entry). The transition was marked by a comprehensive change of social identity: the former vices belonged to the former community membership; the new community membership comes with a new social identity defined by the Spirit and Christ's name.
13: The body-for-food / food-for-body slogan ('food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food') is a Corinthian philosophical axiom about bodily appetites as morally neutral. The Stoics and Epicureans both held versions of the view that bodily appetites (food, sex) were natural and should be managed rationally but were not inherently moral. The Corinthian 'pneumatics' apparently extended this to sexual conduct: as the body will be destroyed, what one does with it is morally inconsequential. Paul's counter-argument shifts the framework entirely: the body is for the Lord, not for immorality, and God will raise it.
14: Paul grounds the body's moral significance in resurrection. In the Greco-Roman context, the body (sōma) was generally regarded as the soul's temporary vehicle, destined for dissolution — hence the indifference to what one did with it. Paul's resurrection-body theology (developed fully in 1 Corinthians 15) directly refutes this: God, who raised the Lord, will also raise us. The body's eschatological future determines its present moral status. This was not an argument available in any form of Greek philosophy; it is Paul's characteristically Jewish-Christian innovation.
15: The 'members of Christ' (melē Christou) language is ecclesiological: the individual body participates in the corporate body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12-27). The horror of making Christ's members into members of a prostitute (pornē) is not merely personal moral failure but a violation of the corporate Christological identity. Prostitution (porneia) in Roman Corinth was commercial, legal, and socially embedded: there were brothels near the agora, and hetairai (educated courtesans) were fixtures of elite male social life. What Paul calls a violation of Christ's body was, to Corinthian social culture, an unremarkable commercial transaction.
16: Paul cites Genesis 2:24 ('the two will become one flesh') to argue that sexual union creates a covenantal bond — not merely a transactional bodily contact. In Jewish exegesis, Genesis 2:24 was foundational for marriage law: the rabbis used it to define the marriage union (m. Ketubbot 1 Corinthians 1:1). By citing it for the union with a prostitute, Paul argues that even a commercial sexual transaction creates a real (if illicit) covenantal bond of the 'one flesh' type. This significantly raises the moral stakes: fornication is not a trivial bodily transaction but a covenantal act with spiritual consequences.
17: The contrast between being 'one flesh' with a prostitute and 'one spirit' with the Lord draws on the Jewish concept of covenant union. In Second Temple Judaism, Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH was described in marital terms (Hosea 2; Ezekiel 16; Song of Songs in its allegorical reading). The 'one spirit' union with the Lord is the positive counterpart to the 'one flesh' bond of sexual union: both are real, both are exclusive, and both are violated by sexual immorality. The spiritual union with Christ is the prior commitment that sexual immorality betrays.
18: The command to 'flee sexual immorality' (pheugete tēn porneian) uses the language of flight common in Jewish moral exhortation. The Joseph story (Genesis 39:12 — Joseph fleeing Potiphar's wife) was a standard example in Jewish moral instruction (T. Joseph 1 Corinthians 9:3; Philo, On Joseph 40-43). The Joseph narrative was frequently invoked in Second Temple Judaism as the model of sexual self-control in a pagan environment — precisely the situation facing Corinthian Christians in a city saturated with commercial prostitution. Flight, not negotiation, is the response to sexual temptation in this tradition.
20: The commercial metaphor of purchase (ēgoriasthēte timēs, you were bought at a price) reflects the Greco-Roman institution of slavery and manumission. Slaves could be bought and freed, but freedom came at a price — either paid by the slave (from savings) or by a patron. Paul's use of redemption-purchase language places believers as those who have been bought by Christ: they belong to him as purchased property, their freedom is freedom-within-ownership. The obligation to 'glorify God in your body' follows from this ownership-transfer: the body that has been purchased belongs to its new owner and must serve his purposes, not the former owner's.
19: The temple of the Holy Spirit (naos tou hagiou pneumatos) builds on the corporate temple-language of 3:16 and applies it to the individual believer. Corinth had numerous actual temples: the Temple of Aphrodite on Acrocorinth (ancient tradition, though contested archaeologically), the Temple of Apollo, and shrines to Asclepius and other deities. The city was saturated with cultic spaces and the social activities associated with them. Paul rhetorically counters this environment by declaring every believer a temple housing a greater divine presence than any of Corinth's famous shrines.
